articles.headline: Your Brain May Be Aging Because Its Protein Factory Is Jamming Up
articles.body: Stanford researchers studying the turquoise killifish — a fish that lives its entire life in just a few months — may have cracked a key reason brains deteriorate with age. They found that ribosomes, the tiny machines inside cells that build proteins, start colliding and stalling as we get older. That causes a chain reaction of faulty proteins and harmful clumps linked to Alzheimer's and other neurodegenerative diseases. The killifish brain showed these jams appearing in learning-related regions before any behavioral decline even emerged. Scientists say this points to protein quality control as a major driver of cognitive aging.
articles.takeaway: You can't fix ribosome jams directly, but exercise, sleep, and stress reduction all support cellular repair systems that keep this machinery running cleaner, longer.

articles.topic: digital behavior change weight loss durability
articles.headline: This App-Based Program Kept Working a Full Year After People Stopped Using It
articles.body: A new randomized controlled trial — the largest ever for Noom — followed 427 adults over 68 weeks. Participants did 16 weeks of active program, then stopped. A year later, the weight loss hadn't just held. It had kept going. Noom users lost an average of 4.1 percent of body weight, while the control group gained 1.5 percent. Noom participants were also more than twice as likely to hit the clinically meaningful 5 percent loss threshold. Researchers say the results support behavior change as a foundational strategy in weight management, not just a short-term fix.
articles.takeaway: Sustainable weight loss is less about the diet and more about changing the habits and thinking patterns around food. Skills built during an intervention can outlast the intervention itself.
articles.pexelsQuery: person tracking health goals on phone app

articles.topic: tomato soy lycopene inflammation reduction clinical trial
articles.headline: A Simple Daily Juice Cut Inflammation Markers in Four Weeks. Here's What Was in It.
articles.body: Ohio State researchers ran a crossover clinical trial giving adults with obesity two small cans of a specially formulated tomato-soy juice every day for four weeks. The juice was loaded with lycopene — the compound that makes tomatoes red — and soy isoflavones. After four weeks, several blood markers of inflammation dropped significantly. When participants switched to a regular low-lycopene tomato juice as a control, those effects disappeared. Chronic low-grade inflammation underlies heart disease, diabetes, and a range of other conditions. This is one of the few human trials to test a food-based approach in a rigorous controlled design.
articles.takeaway: Lycopene from tomatoes and isoflavones from soy are an underrated anti-inflammatory pairing. Cooking tomatoes increases lycopene availability — another reason to add them to regular meals.

articles.topic: melatonin DNA repair night shift workers RCT
articles.headline: Melatonin Might Do More Than Help You Sleep. A New Trial Found It Boosts DNA Repair.
articles.body: Night shift work disrupts the body's melatonin cycle, which weakens its ability to repair oxidative DNA damage — one reason shift workers face higher cancer risk. A randomized placebo-controlled trial from BC Cancer Research involving 40 night shift workers found that taking 3 mg of melatonin before daytime sleep led to 80 percent higher levels of a key DNA repair biomarker compared to placebo. The study is small and preliminary, but it points to a low-cost, widely available supplement potentially doing something important well beyond sleep. Larger trials are underway to confirm the effects and find the optimal dose.
articles.takeaway: If you work nights or have a significantly disrupted sleep schedule, a low-dose melatonin supplement before sleep may offer benefits beyond helping you fall asleep faster.

quote.author: Bessel van der Kolk
quote.context: Psychiatrist and trauma researcher, author of the landmark book on how stress and trauma live in the body.

workout.title: Lunch Break Blast: 3-Minute Interval Repeats
workout.tip: Run these intervals at a pace where speaking more than two or three words feels genuinely hard. If you can chat through them, push a little more.
workout.warmup: Start with 8 minutes of easy jogging — enough to break a light sweat. Add 2 x 20-second strides at the end to wake up your legs before the effort starts.

exercises.name: 3-Minute Hard Interval
exercises.notes: Run at a hard but sustainable effort — about a 7 out of 10. Not a sprint, but genuinely uncomfortable.

exercises.name: 90-Second Easy Jog Recovery
exercises.notes: Keep moving — don't stop or walk unless you really need to. Focus on breathing back down.

workout.cooldown: Finish with 5 minutes of easy jogging, then slow to a walk. Shake out your legs and take a few deep nasal breaths to bring your heart rate back down before you stop completely.

product.name: HOKA Clifton 9
product.blurb: Max cushion for easy days and long runs. Keeps your legs fresh for hard sessions.

meal.title: Seared Beef Sirloin with Roasted Sweet Potato and Arugula Salad
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
meal.instructions: 1. Toss diced sweet potato with half the olive oil, garlic powder, paprika, salt, and pepper. Roast at 425F for 18-20 minutes until golden. 2. Season steak generously with salt and pepper. Sear in a hot cast iron pan for 3-4 minutes per side for medium. Rest for 5 minutes before slicing. 3. Whisk remaining olive oil with lemon juice, Dijon, vinegar, a pinch of salt, and pepper to make a quick dressing. Toss arugula with the dressing. 4. Plate arugula and roasted sweet potato, top with sliced steak and optional feta. Serve immediately.
meal.tip: Let the steak rest fully before slicing — it keeps the juices in and the meat tender. A cast iron pan gives you the best sear with minimal cleanup.

product.name: Greater Goods Digital Food Scale
product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
